summary: Gruber writes a fictional transcript imagining negotiations between Apple's Steve Jobs and Universal Music CEO Doug Morris over a proposed per-iPod royalty fee. Morris, emboldened by his deal to extract $1 per Zune from Microsoft, tries to get $2 per iPod from Apple. Jobs appears to agree enthusiastically, then uses a reductio ad absurdum strategy — suggesting Universal should also get royalties on every Mac sold — before delivering a profane punchline that exposes the absurdity of the entire premise. Morris quickly retreats to the existing 99-cents-per-song deal. The piece satirizes the music industry's sense of entitlement to hardware revenue and highlights the vast power asymmetry between Apple and the labels.
key_points: Universal's Zune royalty deal emboldened the label to seek even larger per-device fees from Apple, framing it as Apple maintaining 'leadership'
Jobs's fictional strategy of enthusiastically agreeing, then extending the logic to Macs, exposes the absurdity of taxing hardware for hypothetical piracy
The power dynamic is starkly illustrated: Morris brings six attorneys and stacks of documents; Jobs brings one attorney and nothing on the table
The Zune's tiny market share is the running joke — seven units would 'double their sales for the week'
The piece argues that the existing iTunes Store deal (99 cents per song) is already fair, and hardware royalties are pure rent-seeking
money_quotes: On the table in front of Morris and each of the Universal attorneys are various neatly stacked folders, contracts, and legal pads. In front of Jobs the table is completely clear; he holds nothing in his hands.
A pittance, really. But what it is, is a small step toward compensating us for the stolen music that belongs to us which we all know is being stored on these sorts of devices.
You can't download music from P2P to an iPod. You download it to your Mac, and then it goes to your iPod. So maybe Universal deserves something for each Mac we sell, too.
Mr. Jobs is suggesting that you take a white Microsoft Zune 30 gigabyte digital music player and insert it into your rectum.
Seven Zunes — that should double their sales for the week.
And Universal Music will get seven dollars.
key_insight: The music industry's demand for per-device royalties on iPods is absurd rent-seeking, and Apple's dominant market position gives it the leverage to simply refuse.
